content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:c7cf7dfd

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: force_memory_write=true; transcript contains durable interest/preference signals plus reusable recommendation history and learning path context.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements about excitement, goals, and intended practice need the stable user entity as subject; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=Traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques; reason=Central reusable concept the user wants to learn and practice.
- mention=traditional Indian clothes and embroideries; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=Traditional Indian clothes and embroideries; reason=Reusable creative output/domain object mentioned as what the user wants to make; distinct enough from techniques to preserve as an object of later claims.
- mention=Indian textiles; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=CONCEPT; name=Indian textiles; reason=Reusable source of design inspiration and learning context.
- mention=Craft Village; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Craft Village; reason=Named workshop provider recommended for traditional Indian embroidery techniques.
- mention=The Block Printing Company; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Block Printing Company; reason=Named workshop provider recommended for Indian textile block printing.
- mention=The Stitching Project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Stitching Project; reason=Named workshop provider recommended for Indian hand embroidery techniques.
- mention=Dharma Trading Co.; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Dharma Trading Co.; reason=Named supplier recommended for materials and tools.
- mention=Fabriclore; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Fabriclore; reason=Named supplier recommended for traditional Indian tailoring materials.
- mention=EmbroideryCentral; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=EmbroideryCentral; reason=Named supplier recommended for embroidery tools and supplies.
- mention=Nostalgia; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Nostalgia; reason=Named online store recommended for materials; generic name but context identifies a craft/textile supplier.
- mention=Amazon; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Amazon; reason=Named marketplace recommended as a source of materials and tools.
- mention=Joann; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Joann; reason=Named craft and fabric store recommended for materials and tools.
- mention=Embroidery Network; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Embroidery Network; reason=Named online community recommended for connecting with embroidery enthusiasts and feedback.
- mention=The Embroidery Resource Forum; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Embroidery Resource Forum; reason=Named online community recommended for questions, sharing work, and feedback.
- mention=Reddit; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Reddit; reason=Named online platform recommended for communities related to embroidery and tailoring.
- mention=Craftsy; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Craftsy; reason=Named online platform recommended for courses and communities.
- mention=Bluprint; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<17>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Bluprint; reason=Mentioned as the current/renamed platform for Craftsy; no existing entity to alias, so create separately to avoid unsafe merge.
- mention=Indian Embroideries; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<18>; type=DOCUMENT; name=Indian Embroideries; reason=Named book recommended for learning; durable document entity.
- mention=John Irwin; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<19>; type=PERSON; name=John Irwin; reason=Named person as book author.
- mention=Margaret Halliday; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<20>; type=PERSON; name=Margaret Halliday; reason=Named person as book author.
- mention=The Art of Indian Asia: Its Mythology and Transformations; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<21>; type=DOCUMENT; name=The Art of Indian Asia: Its Mythology and Transformations; reason=Named book recommended for learning; durable document entity.
- mention=Heinrich Zimmer; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<22>; type=PERSON; name=Heinrich Zimmer; reason=Named person as book author.
- mention=The Fabric of India; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<23>; type=DOCUMENT; name=The Fabric of India; reason=Named book recommended for learning; durable document entity.
- mention=Rosemary Crill; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<24>; type=PERSON; name=Rosemary Crill; reason=Named person as book author.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<25>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/21 (Sun) 20:54
Transcript:
user: How can I learn more about the traditional tailoring and embroidery techniques of India?
assistant: There are several ways to learn more about traditional tailoring and embroidery techniques of India:

1. Take a course or workshop: Search for courses or workshops on traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques offered in your area or consider traveling to India for a more immersive experience.

2. Read books and articles: There are several books on the market that provide detailed information about traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. You can also read articles and blogs related to Indian fashion and embroidery.

3. Visit museums and exhibitions: Visit museums and exhibitions that showcase traditional Indian textile art and embroidery. This can give you an opportunity to see the intricate work up close.

4. Talk to experts: Connect with experts in the field of traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring. You can get in touch with them through online forums, social media, or by attending industry events.

5. Online resources: There are several online resources available that provide detailed information on the traditional techniques, such as YouTube tutorials, blogs, and websites specialized in Indian textiles.
user: Do you have any recommendations for specific books or workshops I should look into for learning about traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques?
assistant: Sure, here are some recommendations for books and workshops related to traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques:

Books:
1. Indian Embroideries by John Irwin and Margaret Halliday
2. The Art of Indian Asia: Its Mythology and Transformations by Heinrich Zimmer
3. The Fabric of India by Rosemary Crill

Workshops:
1. Craft Village: Located in Delhi, India, Craft Village offers workshops and courses on traditional Indian embroidery techniques such as Kantha, Chikankari, and Phulkari.
2. The Block Printing Company: Based in Jaipur, India, The Block Printing Company offers workshops on traditional block printing techniques used in Indian textiles.
3. The Stitching Project: Located in Pushkar, India, The Stitching Project offers workshops on Indian hand embroidery techniques such as Zardozi, Gota Patti, and Mirror Work.

Note: Due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, it's important to check whether the workshops are currently operating or have switched to online formats.
user: These recommendations sound amazing! I'm really excited to learn more about traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. Do you have any advice on how I can get started with practicing these techniques on my own?
assistant: Yes! Here are a few tips that can help you get started with practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques on your own:

1. Start with basic stitches: Most Indian embroidery techniques involve basic stitches like running stitch, chain stitch, and satin stitch. Start by practicing these stitches until you're comfortable with them.

2. Get the right materials: Traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques require specific types of fabrics and threads. Be sure to choose the right materials for your project to ensure the authenticity of the technique you're practicing.

3. Use the right tools: In addition to fabrics and threads, traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques also require specific tools like needles, hoops, and scissors. Make sure you have these tools and use them correctly.

4. Practice regularly: Like any other skill, practicing regularly is key to becoming proficient in traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.

5. Take inspiration from traditional designs: Traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques are often used to create beautiful designs inspired by nature, animals, and geometric shapes. Take inspiration from traditional designs and try to replicate them in your own work.

6. Join a community: Joining an online or local community of embroidery and tailoring enthusiasts can be a great way to get advice, support, and feedback on your work.

Remember, the most important thing is to have fun! Enjoy the process of learning and experimenting with traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.
user: Wow, these tips are really helpful! I am really excited to start practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques on my own. Do you have any recommendations for where I can find high-quality materials and tools?
assistant: Yes, absolutely! Here are a few recommendations for where you can find high-quality materials and tools for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques:

1. Dharma Trading Co. - Dharma Trading Co. specializes in natural dyes, fabrics, and embroidery threads that are perfect for traditional Indian embroidery techniques.

2. Fabriclore - Fabriclore sells a wide range of fabrics like cotton, silk, chiffon, and georgette that can be used for traditional Indian tailoring techniques.

3. EmbroideryCentral - EmbroideryCentral offers a vast selection of embroidery supplies, including embroidery needles, hoops, and frames.

4. Nostalgia - Nostalgia is an online store based in India that offers an expansive collection of traditional Indian fabrics, threads, and embellishments.

5. Amazon - Amazon also offers a wide variety of materials and tools for both Indian embroidery and tailoring, including fabrics, threads, needles, and tools.

6. Joann - Joann is a popular crafting and fabrics store that offers a variety of fabric, thread, and tools suitable for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.

Remember to check for materials and tools that are authentic to the technique you're trying to learn, and try to use high-quality materials to ensure the longevity and authenticity of your work!
user: I'm so excited to get started! I can't wait to make my own traditional Indian clothes and embroideries. Do you have any suggestions for where I can find inspiration for my designs?
assistant: Yes, definitely! There is an abundance of inspiration available for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring designs. Here are a few ideas for where you can find inspiration:

1. Traditional Indian textiles: Look at traditional Indian textiles such as sarees, lehengas, and dupattas to get an idea of the beautiful embroidery and tailoring styles used in India. You can also go through a range of traditional Indian textiles available online which showcase beautiful embroidery, lacework, and prints.

2. Art and architecture: Indian art and architecture incorporate beautiful patterns and motifs that can inspire your embroidery designs. Look for patterns in temples, palaces, and other historical buildings, as well as in paintings and sculptures.

3. Nature: The beautiful scenery and wildlife in India can inspire embroidery designs with beautiful floral or animal motifs. Flowers such as lotus, jasmine, and hibiscus appear in many Indian designs.

4. Social gatherings and festivals: Indian social gatherings and festivals provide an opportunity to see beautiful traditional costumes and embroidered fabrics. Attend festivals and events, such as Holi, Diwali, and Navratri, and take inspiration from the traditional outfits worn by people.

5. Online resources: There are several online resources available that showcase traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring designs. Look at popular websites like Pinterest and Instagram to get ideas and inspiration for your design.

Remember that traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring is all about experimentation and creativity. Play around with different colors, fabrics, and patterns to create your own unique designs!
user: I'm really excited to start practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. Do you have any recommendations for online communities or forums where I can connect with other enthusiasts and get feedback on my work?
assistant: Yes, definitely! Online communities and forums can be a great way to get feedback and advice on your work and connect with other enthusiasts who share your interest in traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. Here are a few recommendations for online communities and forums:

1. Embroidery Network - The Embroidery Network is an online community for embroidery enthusiasts, with a specific section dedicated to Indian embroidery techniques. Here, you can connect with like-minded people, share your work and seek feedback on it.

2. The Embroidery Resource Forum - The Embroidery Resource Forum is an active online community that provides insights into different types of embroidery techniques. Here, you can ask questions, share your work, and get feedback.

3. Reddit - There are several subreddits related to embroidery and traditional Indian tailoring techniques where you can connect with other enthusiasts, share your work, and get feedback from others.

4. Craftsy - Craftsy (now Bluprint) offers a variety of online courses and communities for crafters including embroidery, sewing, and quilting.  The course and forums provide an opportunity to connect with others, share your work, and get tips and advice.

Remember to share your work, ask for feedback, and connect with other enthusiasts! It's a great way to grow your skills and enthusiasm for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.
user: Wow, thank you so much for all of your recommendations and tips! I'm feeling very inspired to start practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. I can't wait to see where my creativity takes me!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. Practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques can be a wonderful and enriching experience. Enjoy the process of learning, experimenting, and creating something beautiful! Good luck, and have fun!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:c7cf7dfd

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: force_memory_write=true; transcript contains durable interest/preference signals plus reusable recommendation history and learning path context.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: How can I learn more about the traditional tailoring and embroidery techniques of India?
assistant: There are several ways to learn more about traditional tailoring and embroidery techniques of India:

1. Take a course or workshop: Search for courses or workshops on traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques offered in your area or consider traveling to India for a more immersive experience.

2. Read books and articles: There are several books on the market that provide detailed information about traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. You can also read articles and blogs related to Indian fashion and embroidery.

3. Visit museums and exhibitions: Visit museums and exhibitions that showcase traditional Indian textile art and embroidery. This can give you an opportunity to see the intricate work up close.

4. Talk to experts: Connect with experts in the field of traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring. You can get in touch with them through online forums, social media, or by attending industry events.

5. Online resources: There are several online resources available that provide detailed information on the traditional techniques, such as YouTube tutorials, blogs, and websites specialized in Indian textiles.
user: Do you have any recommendations for specific books or workshops I should look into for learning about traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques?
assistant: Sure, here are some recommendations for books and workshops related to traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques:

Books:
1. Indian Embroideries by John Irwin and Margaret Halliday
2. The Art of Indian Asia: Its Mythology and Transformations by Heinrich Zimmer
3. The Fabric of India by Rosemary Crill

Workshops:
1. Craft Village: Located in Delhi, India, Craft Village offers workshops and courses on traditional Indian embroidery techniques such as Kantha, Chikankari, and Phulkari.
2. The Block Printing Company: Based in Jaipur, India, The Block Printing Company offers workshops on traditional block printing techniques used in Indian textiles.
3. The Stitching Project: Located in Pushkar, India, The Stitching Project offers workshops on Indian hand embroidery techniques such as Zardozi, Gota Patti, and Mirror Work.

Note: Due to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, it's important to check whether the workshops are currently operating or have switched to online formats.
user: These recommendations sound amazing! I'm really excited to learn more about traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. Do you have any advice on how I can get started with practicing these techniques on my own?
assistant: Yes! Here are a few tips that can help you get started with practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques on your own:

1. Start with basic stitches: Most Indian embroidery techniques involve basic stitches like running stitch, chain stitch, and satin stitch. Start by practicing these stitches until you're comfortable with them.

2. Get the right materials: Traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques require specific types of fabrics and threads. Be sure to choose the right materials for your project to ensure the authenticity of the technique you're practicing.

3. Use the right tools: In addition to fabrics and threads, traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques also require specific tools like needles, hoops, and scissors. Make sure you have these tools and use them correctly.

4. Practice regularly: Like any other skill, practicing regularly is key to becoming proficient in traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.

5. Take inspiration from traditional designs: Traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques are often used to create beautiful designs inspired by nature, animals, and geometric shapes. Take inspiration from traditional designs and try to replicate them in your own work.

6. Join a community: Joining an online or local community of embroidery and tailoring enthusiasts can be a great way to get advice, support, and feedback on your work.

Remember, the most important thing is to have fun! Enjoy the process of learning and experimenting with traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.
user: Wow, these tips are really helpful! I am really excited to start practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques on my own. Do you have any recommendations for where I can find high-quality materials and tools?
assistant: Yes, absolutely! Here are a few recommendations for where you can find high-quality materials and tools for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques:

1. Dharma Trading Co. - Dharma Trading Co. specializes in natural dyes, fabrics, and embroidery threads that are perfect for traditional Indian embroidery techniques.

2. Fabriclore - Fabriclore sells a wide range of fabrics like cotton, silk, chiffon, and georgette that can be used for traditional Indian tailoring techniques.

3. EmbroideryCentral - EmbroideryCentral offers a vast selection of embroidery supplies, including embroidery needles, hoops, and frames.

4. Nostalgia - Nostalgia is an online store based in India that offers an expansive collection of traditional Indian fabrics, threads, and embellishments.

5. Amazon - Amazon also offers a wide variety of materials and tools for both Indian embroidery and tailoring, including fabrics, threads, needles, and tools.

6. Joann - Joann is a popular crafting and fabrics store that offers a variety of fabric, thread, and tools suitable for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.

Remember to check for materials and tools that are authentic to the technique you're trying to learn, and try to use high-quality materials to ensure the longevity and authenticity of your work!
user: I'm so excited to get started! I can't wait to make my own traditional Indian clothes and embroideries. Do you have any suggestions for where I can find inspiration for my designs?
assistant: Yes, definitely! There is an abundance of inspiration available for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring designs. Here are a few ideas for where you can find inspiration:

1. Traditional Indian textiles: Look at traditional Indian textiles such as sarees, lehengas, and dupattas to get an idea of the beautiful embroidery and tailoring styles used in India. You can also go through a range of traditional Indian textiles available online which showcase beautiful embroidery, lacework, and prints.

2. Art and architecture: Indian art and architecture incorporate beautiful patterns and motifs that can inspire your embroidery designs. Look for patterns in temples, palaces, and other historical buildings, as well as in paintings and sculptures.

3. Nature: The beautiful scenery and wildlife in India can inspire embroidery designs with beautiful floral or animal motifs. Flowers such as lotus, jasmine, and hibiscus appear in many Indian designs.

4. Social gatherings and festivals: Indian social gatherings and festivals provide an opportunity to see beautiful traditional costumes and embroidered fabrics. Attend festivals and events, such as Holi, Diwali, and Navratri, and take inspiration from the traditional outfits worn by people.

5. Online resources: There are several online resources available that showcase traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring designs. Look at popular websites like Pinterest and Instagram to get ideas and inspiration for your design.

Remember that traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring is all about experimentation and creativity. Play around with different colors, fabrics, and patterns to create your own unique designs!
user: I'm really excited to start practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. Do you have any recommendations for online communities or forums where I can connect with other enthusiasts and get feedback on my work?
assistant: Yes, definitely! Online communities and forums can be a great way to get feedback and advice on your work and connect with other enthusiasts who share your interest in traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. Here are a few recommendations for online communities and forums:

1. Embroidery Network - The Embroidery Network is an online community for embroidery enthusiasts, with a specific section dedicated to Indian embroidery techniques. Here, you can connect with like-minded people, share your work and seek feedback on it.

2. The Embroidery Resource Forum - The Embroidery Resource Forum is an active online community that provides insights into different types of embroidery techniques. Here, you can ask questions, share your work, and get feedback.

3. Reddit - There are several subreddits related to embroidery and traditional Indian tailoring techniques where you can connect with other enthusiasts, share your work, and get feedback from others.

4. Craftsy - Craftsy (now Bluprint) offers a variety of online courses and communities for crafters including embroidery, sewing, and quilting.  The course and forums provide an opportunity to connect with others, share your work, and get tips and advice.

Remember to share your work, ask for feedback, and connect with other enthusiasts! It's a great way to grow your skills and enthusiasm for traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques.
user: Wow, thank you so much for all of your recommendations and tips! I'm feeling very inspired to start practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques. I can't wait to see where my creativity takes me!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. Practicing traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring techniques can be a wonderful and enriching experience. Enjoy the process of learning, experimenting, and creating something beautiful! Good luck, and have fun!

Recommended workshops were Craft Village in Delhi for Kantha, Chikankari, and Phulkari; The Block Printing Company in Jaipur for Indian textile block printing; and The Stitching Project in Pushkar for Zardozi, Gota Patti, and Mirror Work, with a note to check COVID-19 operating status or online formats.","tags":["recommendation_history","books","workshops","indian_textiles"],"title":"Recommended books and workshops for Indian embroidery and textile techniques","valid_from":"2023-05-21T20:54:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":[],"confidence":0.94,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<2>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<7>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<8>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<9>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<10>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<11>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<12>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<13>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<14>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<15>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<16>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<17>","role":"mentioned"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"Here are a few recommendations for where you can find high-quality materials and tools","evidence_reason":"The transcript contains multiple named resource lists for suppliers, inspiration sources, and communities relevant to the same practice goal.","importance":4,"keywords":["Dharma Trading Co.","Fabriclore","EmbroideryCentral","Nostalgia","Amazon","Joann","Embroidery Network","The Embroidery Resource Forum","Reddit","Craftsy","Bluprint","sarees","lehengas","dupattas","Holi","Diwali","Navratri"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This preserves reusable recommendation context and a resource map; splitting into atomic claims would lose the practical grouping and option-set value.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Resource map for the user's independent practice of traditional Indian embroidery and tailoring"},"summary":"Suggested suppliers for Indian embroidery and tailoring materials/tools were Dharma Trading Co., Fabriclore, EmbroideryCentral, Nostalgia, Amazon, and Joann; inspiration sources included traditional Indian textiles such as sarees, lehengas, and dupattas, Indian art and architecture, nature motifs, festivals like Holi, Diwali, and Navratri, and Pinterest/Instagram. Suggested feedback communities were Embroidery Network, The Embroidery Resource Forum, Reddit, and Craftsy (now Bluprint).","tags":["resource_map","materials","inspiration","communities","indian_embroidery"],"title":"Materials, inspiration, and community resources for Indian embroidery practice","valid_from":"2023-05-21T20:54:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
